meta.label: Ethiopian
meta.description: Communal, spice-forward cooking centered on injera (teff sourdough flatbread) and richly layered stews (wot/wat) built from aromatics, niter kibbeh, and signature blends like berbere and mitmita. Strong lentil/legume tradition and deep roasting/simmering flavors.
meta.subtitle: Injera + wot (stews), berbere heat, niter kibbeh depth, long-simmered comfort
heritageNotes: Many dishes are built for sharing: stews and sautéed dishes are served on a large injera and eaten by hand.
Spice blends and clarified butter are foundational: berbere/mitmita for heat and aroma; niter kibbeh for fat-based flavor.
meta.author: SSA Catalog

source.author: SSA
source.notes: IDs referenced here are SSA-style canonical tokens; map to your actual catalogs via resolver/aliases.

overlapsWith.reason: Shared regional ingredients and some overlapping stew/legume patterns.

subprofiles.name: Highlands
subprofiles.signatureNotes: Teff-forward injera with lentil stews and long-simmered aromatics; strong berbere backbone.

subprofiles.name: Rift Valley
subprofiles.signatureNotes: Greens and sautéed components (e.g., gomen) alongside stews; balanced fat and aromatics.

subprofiles.name: Red (Berbere/Mitmita)
subprofiles.signatureNotes: Heat-forward dishes (e.g., doro wot) with berbere complexity; slower cooking for depth.

plannerDefaults.weeknightBias: Use batch bases (onion reduction, berbere paste, cooked lentils) and quick sauté sides (gomen, cabbage/carrot/potato) to make Ethiopian weeknight-friendly.

batchCookingBias.notes: Ethiopian stews reheat extremely well; cook bases in bulk and build multiple wot variations across the week.

signatureNotes: deep onion sweetness from long reduction

tier0_minimum.description: Build Ethiopian-inspired stews and sides with common pantry items plus one key spice blend.

tier1_core.description: Core Ethiopian pantry for authentic wot structure and injera-adjacent meals.

tier2_traditional.description: Traditional helpers and specialty items for deeper authenticity and variety.

tier3_specialty.description: Specialty items (regional/rare) used occasionally or for deep dives.

notes: The 'wot base' onion reduction is the core engine for many stews.
Spice blooming in fat (niter kibbeh or oil) is essential for aroma release.
Injera is fermented; plan ahead or use a starter/substitute strategy.

dishArchetypes.label: Injera Platter
dishArchetypes.description: A communal platter built on injera with multiple stews/sautés arranged for sharing.

dishArchetypes.label: Wot (Wat) Stew
dishArchetypes.description: Thick, richly spiced stew built on onion reduction and berbere with a fat-based bloom.

dishArchetypes.label: Legume Stew
dishArchetypes.description: Hearty lentil/pea/chickpea stew; can be berbere-forward (red) or milder (yellow).

dishArchetypes.label: Sauté Side (Greens/Veg)
dishArchetypes.description: Quick sautéed vegetable sides balancing rich stews (e.g., garlicky greens).

avoid_or_limit.label: Heavy cream
avoid_or_limit.reason: Most Ethiopian savory dishes build richness through clarified butter and reduction rather than cream.

avoid_or_limit.label: Sweet ketchup-based sauces
avoid_or_limit.reason: Can distort berbere-forward flavor balance; use tomato paste + aromatics for sweetness instead.

proteinSubstitutions.notes: For doro-style stews, poultry is classic; goat/lamb works but increase simmer time until tender.
Tibs and siga wot adapt well to lamb/goat; adjust fat and cook time.

sideSubstitutions.notes: If teff injera is unavailable, use teff-blend injera or a tangy flatbread. Rice can stand in for weeknight plates.

thickenerSubstitutions.notes: Shiro is seasoned chickpea flour; plain chickpea flour works—add berbere/garlic and simmer until glossy.

notes: If niter kibbeh is unavailable, use ghee plus a small spice bloom (garlic/ginger + a pinch of warm spices) to approximate the aroma.
For berbere, a mild chili powder + paprika + warm spices can approximate in a pinch; adjust heat gradually.

mealTemplates.label: Injera Platter (3 stews + 2 sides)

components.notes: Use injera if possible for the full experience.
Batch onion base; split into different stews.
Choose one meat stew OR go vegetarian with more legume stews.
Include at least one legume stew for balance and pantry economy.
Add one mild veg side to balance heat.

prepNotes: Cook a big wot onion base first; then split into 2–3 pots for different stews.
Make injera ahead or source from a local market; keep warm wrapped.

mealTemplates.label: Weeknight Misir Wot Plate
prepNotes: Use pre-made onion base or frozen portions for 25–35 minute cook time.
Cook lentils until silky; finish with lemon if desired.

safetyNotes.label: Injera fermentation & storage
notes: Injera batter ferments at room temperature; use clean containers and avoid contamination.
Cooked injera should be cooled, wrapped, and refrigerated within a few hours; reheat by steaming or warming in a covered pan.

safetyNotes.label: Managing berbere/mitmita heat
notes: Berbere and mitmita can be very hot depending on blend; start small and scale gradually.
Bloom spices in fat gently to prevent scorching, which can create bitterness.

safetyNotes.label: Legume stew thickening
notes: Chickpea flour (shiro-style) thickens as it simmers; keep whisking at first to prevent lumps.
Ensure adequate simmer time to fully cook flour-based stews.

availabilityNotes.note: Use ghee or butter and bloom garlic/ginger + warm spices briefly to approximate niter kibbeh aroma.
For injera-like results without pure teff, use blends; maintain fermentation tang and cook on a hot griddle.
Blend mild chili powder + paprika + warm spices (cumin, clove, cinnamon) if berbere is unavailable; adjust heat slowly.

hallmarkProcesses.name: Wot Onion Base
hallmarkProcesses.notes: Cook onions low and slow until deeply reduced; this forms the base for many stews.

hallmarkProcesses.name: Injera Ferment + Cook
hallmarkProcesses.notes: Injera relies on fermentation for tang and texture; cook with a lid to steam.

signatureMethods: Long onion reduction for stew base.

notes: A heavy pot helps prevent scorching during long onion reductions.
A large lidded pan or griddle helps steam-cook injera properly.

vegan.notes: Many Ethiopian staples are lentil/pea-based; use oil instead of niter kibbeh and focus on misir, shiro, kik alicha, gomen, atkilt.

gluten_free.notes: True teff injera is gluten-free; confirm blends if using wheat/barley. Many stews are naturally GF if thickened without wheat.

dairy_free.notes: Use oil instead of niter kibbeh/butter; the cuisine adapts well for dairy-free households.

core.name: Doro_wot
core.notes: Iconic berbere-forward chicken stew often served with eggs; traditionally slow-simmered for deep flavor.

core.name: Misir_wot
core.notes: Weeknight-friendly, pantry-based lentil stew with berbere and onion base.

core.name: Shiro
core.notes: Creamy stew made from seasoned chickpea flour; fast and highly adaptable.

core.name: Tibs
core.notes: Quick-cooked meat with aromatics and spices; great for weeknights when base is prepped.

core.name: Gomen
core.notes: Greens sautéed with garlic and spiced fat; essential balancing side.

dessertsAndDrinks.name: Spiced_tea
dessertsAndDrinks.notes: Warm spice tea often served alongside meals or after.

typicalFlavorPairings.note: Rich berbere chicken stew pairs best with mild cabbage/potato sides and bright citrus.
Lentil stew is balanced by garlicky greens and fresh salad.

antiPairings.note: Overly sweet sauces can clash with berbere and onion-reduction depth.

ingredientRules.notes: Use ghee as a direct substitute; bloom garlic/ginger + warm spices briefly to mimic niter kibbeh.
Season chickpea flour with berbere, garlic, and salt; whisk into simmering liquid to thicken.
Use tangy flatbread as a practical substitute; serve stews family-style.

methodRules.notes: Cook injera in a wide nonstick pan; pour, cover with lid to steam, remove when set.

batchFriendly.name: Wot Onion Base
batchFriendly.notes: Cook a large onion reduction and freeze in 1–2 cup portions; base for multiple stews.

batchFriendly.name: Berbere Paste
batchFriendly.notes: Mix berbere with aromatics and fat; freeze small portions for quick weeknight stews.

batchFriendly.name: Cooked Lentils
batchFriendly.notes: Cook red lentils plain; use as a fast base for misir wot by adding wot base + berbere.

weeknightShortcuts: Use frozen wot base + canned chickpeas to make a fast shiro-style stew in 20–25 minutes.
Make garlicky greens while a stew simmers; it’s a quick balance dish.
Serve stews with rice or flatbread if injera is not available on a given weeknight.

notes: If your ingredient/equipment catalogs use different IDs, wire aliases in cuisine rulesets or global alias maps.
This profile is designed to work with deterministic planners: batch bases + modular stew/side composition.
Ethiopian cooking is modular: one onion base can become multiple stews by changing spices and main components.
Many staples are legume-forward and can be vegan while still rich and satisfying.
Injera is a key experience component; when unavailable, prioritize tangy/fermented breads or serve as bowls over rice.
